message.content:
muster, then list all rbk-* and jjk-* heats. Present them as two
            plain-text blocks (one headed "RBK heats", one headed "JJK
    heats"),
            each sorted alphabetically by silks. Use space-padded column
    alignment
            — no markdown table pipes or border characters. Columns:
  firemark,
            silks, status, done/total.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content: We do the step above all the time.  Can you propose me a slash command we can keep in this repo to do this?  memorable distinct single word, I think.

attachment.snippet:
1	## File Acronym Mappings — RBK Subdirectory (`Tools/rbk/`)
2	
3	- **RBA**  → `rbk/rba_Auth.sh` (Auth — RBRA/RBRO credential load and role token mint)
4	- **RBDC** → `rbk/rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh`
5	- **RBF**  → Foundry prefix (non-terminal: children rbfc, rbfd, rbfh, rbfk, rbfl, rbfr, rbfv). The former `rbf_Foundry.sh` monolith was decomposed and its residual renamed to `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`; no file is named bare `rbf_`.
6	- **RBFC** → `rbk/rbfc*` (Foundry Core family — the current 1576-line `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` decomposes into the children below at the foundry-explosion pace; these are the allocated targets. Container.)
7	  - **RBFCV** → `rbk/rbfcv_*.sh` (Foundry Core vessel-resolution)
8	  - **RBFCB** → `rbk/rbfcb_*.sh` (Foundry Core build-host primitives — write-script-body, wait-build-completion, native-path, git-metadata; called by the `rblds_` spine)
9	  - **RBFCA** → `rbk/rbfca_*.sh` (Foundry Core step-assembly)
10	  - **RBFCG** → `rbk/rbfcg_*.sh` (Foundry Core GAR-REST)
11	  - **RBFCP** → `rbk/rbfcp_*.sh` (Foundry Core plumb — ~640 lines, the single biggest extraction)
12	  - **RBFCK** → `rbk/rbfck_*.sh` (Foundry Core kindle-entry — single inclusion-guard + kindle + leaked tool-image globals `z_rbfc_tool_*` (copied verbatim); also re-homes the cli dispatch as `rbfck_cli` (`zrbfck_furnish`), retiring bare `rbfc_cli.sh` so `rbfc` names nothing once it has children)
13	- **RBFD** → `rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (Foundry Director Build — Director-side Cloud Build orchestration: `rbfd_ordain`/`rbfd_build`/`rbfd_enshrine`/`rbfd_mirror`/`rbfd_graft`, plus reliquary/quota/registry preflights and `zrbfd_stitch_build_json`; CLI partner `rbfd_cli.sh`)
14	- **RBFH** → `rbk/rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh` (Foundry Hygiene — Dockerfile FROM-line constraint shared by kludge and conjure; thin BCG CLI partner `rbk/rbfh_cli.sh` surfaces the contract via `rbw-fhc`/`rbw-fhv`)
15	- **RBFK** → `rbk/rbfk_kludge.sh` (Foundry Kludge — local vessel image build for development (`rbfk_kludge`); CLI partner `rbfk_cli.sh`, colophon `rbw-fk`)
16	- **RBFL** → `rbk/rbfl*` (Foundry Ledger family — the current ~900-line `rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` decomposes into the children below at the foundry-explosion pace; these are the allocated targets. Container. Sub-letters fixed here; cluster boundaries — which function lands in which file — refine at explosion.)
17	  - **RBFLI** → `rbk/rbfli_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger inscribe — reliquary creation / tool-image mirror gesture)
18	  - **RBFLY** → `rbk/rbfly_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger yoke — reliquary-stamp yoke into vessel regimes)
19	  - **RBFLD** → `rbk/rbfld_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger delete — reliquary / ledger removal gesture)
20	  - **RBFLN** → `rbk/rbfln_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger inventory — ledger listing / enumeration gesture)
21	  - **RBFLW** → `rbk/rbflw_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger wrest — image wrest from registry)
22	  - **RBFLK** → `rbk/rbflk_*.sh` (Foundry Ledger kindle-entry — single inclusion-guard + kindle/sentinel; also re-homes the cli dispatch as `rbflk_cli` (`zrbflk_furnish`), retiring bare `rbfl_cli.sh` so `rbfl` names nothing once it has children)
23	- **RBFR** → `rbk/rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh` (Foundry Retriever — retriever-credentialed hallmark summon (`rbfr_summon`); CLI partner `rbfr_cli.sh`)
24	- **RBFV** → `rbk/rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` (Foundry Verify — hallmark provenance verification: `rbfv_vouch`/`rbfv_about`/`rbfv_vouch_gate`/`rbfv_batch_vouch`; CLI partner `rbfv_cli.sh`)
25	- **RBGA** → `rbk/rbga_ArtifactRegistry.sh`
26	- **RBGB** → `rbk/rbgb_Buckets.sh`
27	- **RBGC** → `rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh`
28	- **RBGE** → `rbk/rbge_Rest.sh` (Google REST — LRO polling + API-enable patterns over rbuh)
29	- **RBGG** → `rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`
30	- **RBGI** → `rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`
31	- **RBGO** → `rbk/rbgo_OAuth.sh` (also owns the stateless `rbgo_curl_status_is_transient_predicate` transient-curl-exit classifier)
32	- **RBGP** → `rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh`
33	- **RBGV** → `rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` (Google Verification — JWT SA and Payor OAuth access probes)
34	- **RBH0** → `rbk/rbh0/` (Handbook directory — decomposed one-function-per-file)
35	  - `rbh*` is the Handbook family (human-facing procedures), parallel to `rbg*` (Google machinery). Three groups: `RBHO` onboarding, `RBHP` payor, `RBHW` windows. Colophon pattern: `rbw-h0` (HandbookTOP index), `rbw-h{o,p,w}` (group tops), `rbw-H{O,P,W}*` (subordinate procedures).
36	  - **RBHO0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbho0_cli.sh` (Onboarding CLI — thin furnish, probe-only dependencies)
37	  - **RBHOB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhob_base.sh` (Onboarding base — kindle, sentinel, probes, shared helpers)
38	  - **RBHP0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhp0_cli.sh` (Payor CLI — full regime + OAuth + IAM dependencies)
39	  - **RBHPB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhpb_base.sh` (Payor base — kindle, sentinel, enforce)
40	  - **RBHW0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_cli.sh` (Windows CLI — handbook display + zipper dependencies)
41	  - **RBHWB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhwb_base.sh` (Windows base — kindle, sentinel)
42	- **RBID** → `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/` (Ifrit — in-bottle attack binary for crucible testing; shared source across tether/airgap variants)
43	- **RBJ**  → Jailer prefix (non-terminal: children rbjs, rbjp, rbje, rbjh)
44	- **RBJS** → `rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh` (Jailer Sentry - container security setup: iptables, dnsmasq, enclave network)
45	- **RBLD** → `rbk/rbld*` (Lode capture family — fetched-side universal capture; cloud-side in-pool steps in `rbgjl/`; GAR namespace `rbi_ld`; colophon family `rbw-l*`; spec letter `RBSL` reserved, file not yet written. Container: the current `rbld_Lode.sh` decomposes into the children below at the host-explosion pace — these are the allocated targets, the files land there.)
46	  - **RBLDS** → `rbk/rblds_*.sh` (Lode capture-assembly spine — composes a build from a recipe + substitutions blob, submits, polls, optionally extracts buildStepOutputs into per-capture capture-files; takes the recipe as data, owns no kind knowledge)
47	  - **RBLDB** → `rbk/rbldb_*.sh` (Bole body — thin ensconce recipe + substitutions + envelope intent riding the spine)
48	  - **RBLDL** → `rbk/rbldl_*.sh` (Lode lifecycle REST — `divine`/`banish`, direct GAR-REST host ops, kept distinct from the assembly spine)
49	  - **RBLDK** → `rbk/rbldk_*.sh` (Lode kindle-entry — single inclusion-guard + kindle, readonly consts read globally; also re-homes the cli dispatch as `rbldk_cli` (`zrbldk_furnish`), retiring bare `rbld_cli.sh` so `rbld` names nothing once it has children)
50	  - Reserved (legend only, no files this heat — future Lode kinds, letters matching the Lode GAR kind-letters): **RBLDT** tool, **RBLDR** reliquary/conclave, **RBLDW** wsl, **RBLDV** podvm
51	- **RBLM** → `rbk/rblm_cli.sh` (Lifecycle Marshal - zero regime to blank template, proof repo for release testing)
52	- **RBNNH** → `rbnnh_` prefix family — optional per-nameplate customization files in `.rbk/{moniker}/`: `rbnnh_compose.yml` (Compose overlay fragment) and `rbnnh_post_charge.sh` (post-charge hook script). Contract: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSCH-charge_hook.adoc`.
53	- **RBOB** → `rbk/rbob_bottle.sh`
54	- **RBYC** → `rbk/rbyc_common.sh` (Common vocabulary — linked term constants for handbook yelp fragments)
55	- **RBQ**  → `rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` (Qualification orchestrator - tabtarget/colophon/nameplate health)
56	- **RBTD** → `rbk/rbtd/` (Theurge — crucible test orchestrator)
57	- **RBTW** → `rbk/rbtd/rbtw_workbench.sh` (Theurge workbench — build/test routing, orthogonal from VOW)
58	- **RBUH** → `rbk/rbuh_Http.sh` (Utility HTTP — JSON REST, polling, shared temp-file machinery)
59	- **RBS0** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc`
60	- **RBSAA** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAA-ark_abjure.adoc`
61	- **RBSAB** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAB-ark_about.adoc` (Standalone Cloud Build about pipeline - syft SBOM + mode-aware build_info)
62	- **RBSAC** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`
63	- **RBSAE** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc`
64	- **RBSAG** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAG-ark_graft.adoc` (Graft operation - local image push to GAR)
65	- **RBSAJ** → `rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`

content.text: Let me see the format convention these follow.

content.content:
1	---
2	argument-hint: [message]
3	description: Guarded git commit
4	---
5	
6	Create a guarded git commit via VVK infrastructure.
7	
8	Arguments: $ARGUMENTS (optional commit message; if omitted, Claude generates from diff)
9	
10	Use this command for non-JJ repositories or when you don't need heat/pace context.
11	For JJ-aware commits, use `/jjc-pace-notch` instead.
12	
13	## Step 0: JJ context detection
14	
15	If you have an active JJ heat context (PACE_CORONET stored from `/jjc-heat-mount`):
16	
17	- Warn the user: "You have an active JJ heat context. Consider using `/jjc-pace-notch` to include pace tracking in the commit."
18	- Ask: "Proceed with plain commit anyway, or use JJ-aware commit?"
19	- If user chooses JJ-aware: run `/jjc-pace-notch` instead
20	
21	If no JJ context, proceed silently.
22	
23	## Step 1: Execute commit (in background)
24	
25	Run the commit command in the background using `run_in_background: true`.
26	
27	**If $ARGUMENTS provided (user gave message):**
28	```bash
29	./tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh vvx_commit --message "<$ARGUMENTS>"
30	```
31	
32	**If $ARGUMENTS empty (generate message):**
33	```bash
34	./tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh vvx_commit
35	```
36	
37	The `vvx_commit` command will:
38	1. Acquire lock (`refs/vvg/locks/vvx`)
39	2. Stage all files including untracked (`git add -A`)
40	3. Run size guard (reject if staged changes too large)
41	4. Generate commit message from diff using `claude --print` (if not provided)
42	5. Commit with Co-Authored-By trailer
43	6. Release lock
44	
45	## Step 2: Report result
46	
47	Use `TaskOutput` to retrieve the background task result, then report:
48	
49	On success:
50	- Commit hash (from stdout, line starting with hex characters)
51	- Commit size (from stderr, extract bytes from `guard: OK - staged content N bytes`)
52	- "Push when ready: `git push`"
53	
54	Example output format:
55	```
56	Commit: abc123def
57	Size: 12,345 bytes
58	Push when ready: `git push`
59	```
60	
61	On failure, report the error from vvx.
62	
63	## Commit message format
64	
65	The commit will be formatted as:
66	```
67	<message>
68	
69	Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
70	```
71	
72	## Size guard
73	
74	If staged changes exceed the configured limit, the commit will be rejected.
75	This prevents accidentally committing large binary files or generated content.
76	
77	## Error handling
78	
79	Common errors:
80	- "Lock held" — another vvx operation is in progress; wait and retry
81	- "Size guard failed" — staged changes too large; unstage large files
82	- "claude CLI not found" — install claude CLI or provide --message
83	
84	## Available Operations
85	
86	- `/vvc-commit` — Guarded git commit (this command)
87	- `/jjc-pace-notch` — JJ-aware commit with heat/pace context

file.content:
---
argument-hint: [message]
description: Guarded git commit
---

Create a guarded git commit via VVK infrastructure.

Arguments: $ARGUMENTS (optional commit message; if omitted, Claude generates from diff)

Use this command for non-JJ repositories or when you don't need heat/pace context.
For JJ-aware commits, use `/jjc-pace-notch` instead.

## Step 0: JJ context detection

If you have an active JJ heat context (PACE_CORONET stored from `/jjc-heat-mount`):

- Warn the user: "You have an active JJ heat context. Consider using `/jjc-pace-notch` to include pace tracking in the commit."
- Ask: "Proceed with plain commit anyway, or use JJ-aware commit?"
- If user chooses JJ-aware: run `/jjc-pace-notch` instead

If no JJ context, proceed silently.

## Step 1: Execute commit (in background)

Run the commit command in the background using `run_in_background: true`.

**If $ARGUMENTS provided (user gave message):**
```bash
./tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh vvx_commit --message "<$ARGUMENTS>"
```

**If $ARGUMENTS empty (generate message):**
```bash
./tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh vvx_commit
```

The `vvx_commit` command will:
1. Acquire lock (`refs/vvg/locks/vvx`)
2. Stage all files including untracked (`git add -A`)
3. Run size guard (reject if staged changes too large)
4. Generate commit message from diff using `claude --print` (if not provided)
5. Commit with Co-Authored-By trailer
6. Release lock

## Step 2: Report result

Use `TaskOutput` to retrieve the background task result, then report:

On success:
- Commit hash (from stdout, line starting with hex characters)
- Commit size (from stderr, extract bytes from `guard: OK - staged content N bytes`)
- "Push when ready: `git push`"

Example output format:
```
Commit: abc123def
Size: 12,345 bytes
Push when ready: `git push`
```

On failure, report the error from vvx.

## Commit message format

The commit will be formatted as:
```
<message>

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

## Size guard

If staged changes exceed the configured limit, the commit will be rejected.
This prevents accidentally committing large binary files or generated content.

## Error handling

Common errors:
- "Lock held" — another vvx operation is in progress; wait and retry
- "Size guard failed" — staged changes too large; unstage large files
- "claude CLI not found" — install claude CLI or provide --message

## Available Operations

- `/vvc-commit` — Guarded git commit (this command)
- `/jjc-pace-notch` — JJ-aware commit with heat/pace context

lastPrompt: We do the step above all the time.  Can you propose me a slash command we can keep in this repo to do this?  memorable distinct single word, I think.

aiTitle: List RBK and JJK heats with formatted output

content.text:
Grounded now. Here's how slash commands live in this repo: `.claude/commands/<name>.md`, YAML frontmatter (`description`, optional `argument-hint`), then a stepwise markdown body. The existing four all follow the mint's `{prefix}-{noun}` shape (`vvc-commit`, `cma-prep-pr`, and they reference `/jjc-…` for JJK ops).

That surfaces one honest tension worth naming before you pick: your own mint discipline puts slash commands in the Primary Universe as `/{prefix}-{noun}` — a JJK-data view would be `/jjc-<noun>`. A bare single word is a deliberate exception to that rule. It's your call, and "I think" tells me you're open — I just don't want to quietly route around a convention you authored.

Here's the body I'd put behind whatever word you choose:

```markdown
---
description: Corral heats by kit — grouped, sorted, space-padded lineup
---

Show all RBK and JJK heats as two plain-text blocks.

## Step 1: Ensure officium
If no officium is open this session, call `jjx_open` alone, capture the ☉-id.
Otherwise reuse the open one.

## Step 2: Muster
Call `jjx_list` (no status filter) with the officium and your verbatim model id.

## Step 3: Partition + sort
- "RBK heats": rows whose silks begin `rbk-`.
- "JJK heats": rows whose silks begin `jjk-`.
- Drop every other heat (`rbw-`, `vok-`, `apck-`, …). Do not coerce near-misses.
- Sort each block alphabetically by silks (plain string order).

## Step 4: Render
Two fenced plain-text blocks, headed `RBK heats` and `JJK heats`.
Space-padded columns, no table pipes or border chars.
Columns: firemark, silks, status, done/total (e.g. `46/57`).
- Always print full firemarks with the ₣ glyph; never abbreviate (display discipline).
- Left-justify firemark and silks.

If a heat looks like it should belong but was excluded, flag it in one line.
Otherwise report nothing else.
```

On the word itself — my pick and reasoning below. Any of these can also wear the compliant `/jjc-` prefix (e.g. `/jjc-corral`) if you'd rather honor the rule; "Other" lets you type your own.

questions.question: Which single word should name the command (file becomes .claude/commands/<word>.md, invoked as /<word>)?

options.label: corral
options.description: Equestrian, fits JJK's horse vocabulary, and I believe unused in any JJK verb or RBK colophon. Means 'gather the herd into pens' — here, exactly two pens. Most precise to the operation.

options.label: roster
options.description: A roster IS a formatted list of names + status — semantically on-the-nose. Downside: faintly collides with RBK's existing 'roster' SA verbs (GovernorRostersRetrievers), so the word already carries a meaning in this repo.

options.label: herd
options.description: Gather the herd. Short, vivid, equestrian. Slightly less precise than corral — it names the animals, not the grouping-into-pens action.

input.content:
---
description: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
---

Muster the heats and roster the `rbk-*` and `jjk-*` families as two plain-text
blocks. Reproduces the standing lineup view.

## Step 1: Ensure officium
If no officium is open this session, call `jjx_open` **alone** (never co-batched)
and capture the ☉-id. Otherwise reuse the open one.

## Step 2: Muster
Call `jjx_list` (no status filter — all heats) with the officium and your
verbatim model id.

## Step 3: Partition + sort
- **RBK heats**: rows whose silks begin `rbk-`.
- **JJK heats**: rows whose silks begin `jjk-`.
- Drop every other heat (`rbw-`, `vok-`, `apck-`, …). Do not coerce near-misses;
  if you drop something that looks like it should belong, flag it in one line.
- Sort each block alphabetically by silks (plain string order).

## Step 4: Render
Two fenced plain-text blocks, one headed `RBK heats`, one headed `JJK heats`.
Space-padded columns — **no markdown table pipes or border characters.**
Columns, in order: firemark, silks, status, done/total (e.g. `46/57`).

- Always print full firemarks with the ₣ glyph; never abbreviate (display discipline).
- Left-justify firemark and silks; status and progress follow.

Report nothing else beyond the two blocks (plus any one-line exclusion flag from Step 3).

---
description: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
---

Muster the heats and roster the `rbk-*` and `jjk-*` families as two plain-text
blocks. Reproduces the standing lineup view.

## Step 1: Ensure officium
If no officium is open this session, call `jjx_open` **alone** (never co-batched)
and capture the ☉-id. Otherwise reuse the open one.

## Step 2: Muster
Call `jjx_list` (no status filter — all heats) with the officium and your
verbatim model id.

## Step 3: Partition + sort
- **RBK heats**: rows whose silks begin `rbk-`.
- **JJK heats**: rows whose silks begin `jjk-`.
- Drop every other heat (`rbw-`, `vok-`, `apck-`, …). Do not coerce near-misses;
  if you drop something that looks like it should belong, flag it in one line.
- Sort each block alphabetically by silks (plain string order).

## Step 4: Render
Two fenced plain-text blocks, one headed `RBK heats`, one headed `JJK heats`.
Space-padded columns — **no markdown table pipes or border characters.**
Columns, in order: firemark, silks, status, done/total (e.g. `46/57`).

- Always print full firemarks with the ₣ glyph; never abbreviate (display discipline).

Report nothing else beyond the two blocks (plus any one-line exclusion flag from Step 3).

content.text:
Written: `.claude/commands/jjk-roster.md` → invoke as `/jjk-roster`.

It captures the exact first-step spec: `jjx_open` (alone) → `jjx_list` → partition into `rbk-*` / `jjk-*` (dropping `rbw-` and the rest), sort each alphabetically by silks, render two fenced space-padded blocks with columns firemark · silks · status · done/total, full `₣` firemarks never abbreviated.

Two honest notes:
- The name `jjk-roster` sits right inside your mint convention — `jjk-` is a clean colophon and `roster` is the noun. The faint "roster" overlap with the Governor SA-rostering verbs I flagged earlier is real but lives in a different namespace (slash command vs `rbw-` colophon), so it shouldn't bite in practice.
- It's a new repo file — uncommitted. Want me to `jjx_record` it (and if so, under which heat/firemark), or leave it for you to commit?
